access.entityLabel: General Corpus source XML for LayCal.lineated

rights.basis: The pinned CME submodule carries an Unlicense worded for software, while the General Corpus README separately licenses code under GPL-3.0 and non-public-domain output under CC BY-SA 4.0. The imported XML also preserves an older personal-scholarly-use notice, and its identified OTA source now has CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 terms. No file-specific statement reconciles those layers, so no single repository license is asserted as controlling every textual and markup component.
rights.component: Checked-in XML serialization, transcription, and modern encoded labor
notes: The medieval text's age does not automatically resolve rights in modern editorial selection, apparatus, markup, or repository-created output.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for LayCal.lineated
notes: The pinned file is 1,119,518 bytes.
It contains 16,096 numbered lines, 161 supplied navigation groups, 418 source-edition page milestones, and a final Amen in CLOSER.

access.accessMethod: Use the stable UMDL handle or item table of contents; the automated research client received HTTP 403 while indexed public pages remained discoverable.
access.entityLabel: University of Michigan CME digital text LayCal
access.provider: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections

rights.basis: The current item page states that the materials are in the public domain and displays the DPLA No Copyright-United States statement. The scope of that display over independently protectable markup and metadata is not separately explained.
rights.component: Current U-M LayCal digital-text presentation and represented materials
notes: A United States no-copyright statement is not a worldwide open license and does not license manuscript photographs or the modern source edition.

rights.basis: The checked-in U-M header says the text was made available through OTA ‘for personal scholarly use only.’ This historical restriction conflicts in scope with the current U-M No Copyright-United States display and current OTA CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 deposit license; no item-specific reconciliation was found.
rights.component: Legacy availability notice embedded in the 1993 U-M XML header
notes: The notice is preserved as historically explicit evidence, not silently treated as revoked or as controlling unrelated manuscript images and editions.

entities.description: The public CME presentation and structured XML transformation of the Caligula stream. It numbers 16,096 verse lines, supplies 100-line navigation groups and Brook-Leslie page milestones, converts most OTA custom entities to Unicode, and moves the terminal Amen outside the numbered verse sequence.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME digital text LayCal
notes: The header says front and back matter and editorial notes from the printed edition were omitted; a printed facsimile was also not represented.

Against the first 16,096 OTA line rows, 16,093 are identical after whitespace normalization and custom-entity conversion. The three remaining normalized differences are at lines 71, 1715, and 13643; the exact cause and stage of those changes are undocumented.

rights.basis: The current item page states that the materials are in the public domain and displays the DPLA No Copyright-United States statement. The scope of that display over independently protectable markup and metadata is not separately explained.

workPortion.label: Caligula payload within complete deposit
notes: The same container also retains the sibling layotho-1682.txt payload; the header is a separate public bitstream.

rights.basis: The current OTA record and public TEI header distribute deposit 1682 under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 Unported.
rights.component: OTA 1682 Caligula deposit files
notes: The noncommercial restriction matters for commercial/POD workflows. This deposit-layer license is not transferred to Brook-Leslie page images, the manuscripts, or BL photographs.

entities.description: The complete SGML-like Caligula stream in OTA deposit 1682. It contains 16,097 l rows: the 16,096 Brook-Leslie-numbered text lines plus a final Amen. Custom entities preserve Middle English characters, and inline symbols record manuscript line breaks, additions, loss, editorial supply, and Madden-derived readings.
entities.label: Oxford Text Archive deposit 1682, Caligula payload
notes: The current allzip deliverable contains exactly laycal-1682.txt and layotho-1682.txt; the public hidden header is a separate download.
The deposit header identifies Brook and Leslie's edition and a two-file Caligula/Otho map.

rights.basis: The current OTA record and public TEI header distribute deposit 1682 under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 Unported.
rights.component: OTA 1682 Caligula deposit files
notes: The noncommercial restriction matters for commercial/POD workflows. This deposit-layer license is not transferred to Brook-Leslie page images, the manuscripts, or BL photographs.

entities.description: The sibling Otho-version file in deposit 1682. It encodes the Otho stream of Brook and Leslie's edition and is preserved for comparison, but it is not upstream of the whole Caligula stream represented by LayCal.lineated.
entities.label: Oxford Text Archive deposit 1682, Otho payload
notes: The Otho version is abridged and reworked and survives in a fire-damaged manuscript. Its separate payload prevents the two versions from being conflated.

rights.basis: The Otho payload belongs to the same current OTA deposit distributed under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 Unported.

access.accessMethod: Locate the two volumes through CiNii or WorldCat and request them from a holding library or via interlibrary loan.
access.entityLabel: G. L. Brook and R. F. Leslie, eds., Laȝamon: Brut (1963-1978)

bibliographic.citation: Laȝamon: Brut, ed. G. L. Brook and R. F. Leslie, 2 vols., Early English Text Society, Original Series 250 and 277 (London: published for the Early English Text Society by Oxford University Press, 1963-1978).
bibliographic.publisher: Published for the Early English Text Society by Oxford University Press
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 250, 277
bibliographic.title: Laȝamon: Brut

entities.description: The immediate two-volume source edition. It edits separate Caligula and Otho textual streams from Cotton Caligula A IX and Cotton Otho C XIII. The electronic Caligula payload selects the Caligula stream, not the edition's complete two-version presentation or its editorial matter.
entities.label: G. L. Brook and R. F. Leslie, eds., Laȝamon: Brut (1963-1978)
notes: The XML source statement says the printed work contained a facsimile not represented electronically, but the exact plate count and target are not established from the restricted scan metadata.
A controlled-borrowing scan of volume 1 exists at Internet Archive; no complete public downloadable copy of both volumes was verified.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1963 and 1978 and no open license or public-domain assertion for its modern editorial contributions was found. The available volume-1 scan is controlled/print-disabled rather than openly downloadable.
rights.component: Modern editorial text, apparatus, introductions, notes, and facsimile matter in the 1963 and 1978 volumes
notes: No exact term expiry is inferred because relevant authorship, employer, and publication facts were not fully established.

bibliographic.citation: Laȝamons Brut, or Chronicle of Britain: A Poetical Semi-Saxon Paraphrase of the Brut of Wace, ed. Frederic Madden, 3 vols. (London: Society of Antiquaries of London, 1847).
bibliographic.title: Laȝamons Brut, or Chronicle of Britain

entities.description: Madden's three-volume parallel presentation of the Caligula and Otho manuscript texts with literal translation, notes, and glossary. The later electronic header expressly attributes readings inside single parentheses—readings no longer legible in the manuscripts—to this edition.
entities.label: Frederic Madden, ed., Laȝamons Brut, or Chronicle of Britain (1847)

rights.basis: The three-volume edition was published in 1847 and is historical public-domain material in the United States.
rights.component: Underlying 1847 publication text, translation, notes, and glossary

access.accessMethod: Download the original Google PDF for each of the three Internet Archive items.
access.entityLabel: Internet Archive / Google scans of Madden's 1847 edition

workPortion.label: Caligula column/stream in Madden's published volume 1
locators: Caligula stream opening through Madden line 10229
notes: Line numbers are Madden's edition lineation. The same complete volume also prints the parallel Otho stream, literal translation, and editorial matter.

workPortion.label: Caligula column/stream in Madden's published volume 2
locators: Caligula stream begins with Madden line 10230 and ends before Madden line 25275
notes: Line numbers are Madden's edition lineation. The same complete volume also prints the parallel Otho stream, literal translation, and editorial matter.

workPortion.label: Caligula column/stream in Madden's published volume 3
locators: Caligula stream begins with Madden line 25275 and continues to the end; notes and glossary follow
notes: Line numbers are Madden's edition lineation. The same complete volume also prints the parallel Otho stream, literal translation, and editorial matter.

rights.basis: The underlying 1847 books are public-domain material. The PDFs' embedded Google usage pages request personal, noncommercial use and attribution and warn that public-domain status varies by country; the inspected Internet Archive item metadata supplies no separate scan-layer open license or rights field.
rights.component: Google-produced PDF reproductions and Internet Archive delivery layer
notes: The embedded provider request belongs to the delivered scan layer, not to Madden's 1847 edition or the medieval text; public download availability is not converted into an unrestricted license for the files.

entities.description: Three original Google Books PDF derivatives, together reproducing all three published volumes. The scans retain the parallel manuscript text, translation, notes, and glossary used to verify Madden's supporting role.

rights.basis: The underlying 1847 books are public-domain material. access.accessMethod: Register for a British Library Reader Pass and request the shelfmark through the online collection-item request form; manuscripts and archives must be consulted in London.
access.entityLabel: British Library Cotton MS Caligula A IX

access.accessMethod: Ask British Library digitisation staff about newly supplied images of the witness and written terms covering the intended reuse.
access.entityLabel: British Library Cotton MS Caligula A IX

dateStatements.label: manuscript production
dateStatements.value: Second half of the thirteenth century (1250-1299)

historical_designations: British Museum MS Cotton Caligula A.IX

entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Caligula A IX

physicalDescription.dimensions: approximately 215 × 150 mm; text space approximately 170 × 115 mm
physicalDescription.extent: 261 folios, with two modern endleaves and flyleaves; Laȝamon's Brut at ff. 3r-194v
physicalDescription.format: Composite codex written in two columns
notes: Origin assigned to England, south-west Midlands.
The Brut is the most complete surviving copy and opens at f. 3r with the red rubric ‘Incipit historia brutonum.’
The codex also contains unrelated Middle English and Anglo-Norman works outside this work record.

rights.basis: The represented manuscript and text were produced in the thirteenth century. This status is separate from ownership, physical access, photography, and provider terms.
rights.component: Underlying medieval text and manuscript content, excluding modern photography and catalog text

access.accessMethod: Consult the current Archives and Manuscripts catalog record.
access.entityLabel: British Library Cotton MS Otho C XIII

access.accessMethod: Restrictions apply. Register for a Reader Pass, request the shelfmark through the online form, and consult British Library staff before travel; manuscripts and archives are consulted in London.
access.entityLabel: British Library Cotton MS Otho C XIII

access.accessMethod: No current digitised-content link or complete public facsimile was verified; ask the British Library whether legacy photography, microfilm, or a reproduction order is available.
access.entityLabel: British Library Cotton MS Otho C XIII
access.format: Potential commissioned images or legacy surrogate

dateStatements.label: manuscript production
dateStatements.value: Fourth quarter of the thirteenth century (1275-1299)

entities.description: The later Otho witness, transmitting an abridged and reworked version with modernized diction as well as accidental losses from fire. It is a separate version witness and comparison source, not the source of the complete Caligula stream.
entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Otho C XIII

physicalDescription.format: Manuscript of the Otho version of Laȝamon's Brut
notes: Leaves were damaged in the 1731 Cotton Library fire; the manuscript was rebound by the British Museum in 1962.

rights.basis: The represented manuscript and text were produced in the thirteenth century. This status is separate from ownership, access restrictions, fire-damage documentation, photography, and provider terms.
rights.component: Underlying medieval text and manuscript content, excluding any modern photography and catalog text

notes: ZIP contains the exact provider manifest, an exact-source inventory, and all 543 canvas images.
542 image requests used the supported 1200-pixel-width profile; the spine's requested 1200-pixel URL was rejected and the effective full-size fallback is preserved in the inventory.

workPortion.label: Laȝamon's Brut, Caligula version
notes: The complete present codex is cached; the represented work occupies 384 manuscript-side canvases. Visual inspection confirmed the f. 3r opening rubric and f. 194v endpoint.

rights.basis: The exact IIIF manifest's Usage terms say ‘Public Domain in most countries other than the UK.’ Its machine-actionable rights URL points to a now-missing BL help page, while current BL terms say that content may have item-specific public-domain, Creative Commons, or other conditions.

rights.jurisdiction: Most countries other than the United Kingdom

next_steps: Consult both printed volumes' introductions, apparatus, and plate lists through a holding library or controlled borrowing.
Map every electronic symbol category to the edition's exact conventions without assuming all Caligula supplies derive from Otho.
Identify the omitted facsimile's exact target, extent, reproduction source, and rights before modeling it as a separate facsimile entity.
notes: The current restricted scan metadata establishes access and edition identity but not the missing editorial detail.
openQuestions.question: What exact base-text, collation, supply, normalization, and apparatus rules did Brook and Leslie use for each Caligula passage, and what manuscript surface or plate(s) comprised the facsimile omitted from the electronic text?

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections for item-level production metadata, source files, and correction history.
Compare the three readings against Brook-Leslie and any preserved earlier OTA copy.
Do not silently correct the repository XML until the responsible layer and preferred reading are established.
openQuestions.question: Who transformed OTA 1682 into the 1993 U-M item, from which exact source copy, and why do U-M lines 71, 1715, and 13643 differ after normalization from the current OTA payload?

next_steps: Write to licensing@bl.uk with the exact IIIF manifest, cached derivative profile, intended repository reference use, and possible commercial/POD workflows.
Ask whether the manifest's ‘Public Domain in most countries other than the UK’ statement permits redistribution outside the UK and what UK restrictions remain.
Preserve the written response and update both access and rights records before publishing cached images.
notes: The current manifest rights URI returns 404; public access and local caching are not substituted for permission.
openQuestions.question: What current British Library terms govern the Caligula IIIF photographs in the UK and for Git redistribution, research publication, self-publishing, or commercial print-on-demand?

next_steps: Ask British Library Reference Services and licensing staff about unindexed or legacy photography and complete-object reproduction options.
If imaging is commissioned, request the entire present object, including binding, endleaves, flyleaves, blanks, fragments, and every surviving leaf—not only text-bearing Brut portions.
After receipt, map exact Otho work folios/canvases and the OTA/Brook-Leslie passages separately.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete current-object facsimile, legacy microfilm, or licensed image set of fire-damaged Cotton MS Otho C XIII be obtained?

next_steps: Request item-level clarification from U-M and OTA about the current rights status of transcription and markup.
Ask the CME and General Corpus maintainers to define the intended scope of their repository licenses over imported XML.
Keep medieval text, modern edition, OTA deposit, U-M encoding, repository serialization, and generated outputs as separate rights components until clarified.
openQuestions.question: How should the 1993 personal-scholarly-use notice, current U-M No Copyright-United States display, current OTA CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 license, CME software-worded Unlicense, and General Corpus output license be reconciled for redistribution of this exact XML?

reviewNotes: Primary path: repository XML → U-M LayCal → OTA 1682 Caligula payload → Brook-Leslie Caligula stream → Cotton Caligula A IX.
Supporting path: parenthetical readings no longer legible in the manuscripts are explicitly attributed to Madden 1847; this does not make Madden the base for the whole electronic text.
Otho is a separate version and sibling edition/deposit stream. It is modeled for context without treating it as an unrestricted source of Caligula wording.
Complete-codex caching and public image access do not themselves grant redistribution or commercial-print rights.

lineage.summary: The repository XML represents the Caligula version of Laȝamon's Brut, not an undifferentiated text of both surviving versions. Its direct path is the General Corpus file copied from the 1993 U-M CME item LayCal, which derives from the Caligula file in Oxford Text Archive deposit 1682, which in turn encodes the Caligula stream of G. L. Brook and R. F. Leslie's two-volume 1963-1978 EETS edition, transcribed from British Library Cotton MS Caligula A IX. The deposit separately contains an Otho stream, and the source edition separately edits the later abridged and reworked Otho version from Cotton MS Otho C XIII; neither is made a coequal source of every Caligula reading. The electronic header explicitly says that parenthetical readings no longer legible in the manuscripts came from Frederic Madden's 1847 parallel edition, making Madden a passage-scoped supporting source. The current complete 543-canvas British Library IIIF surrogate is cached with the Caligula work mapped to ff. 3r-194v, canvases 15-398. Editions, encodings, witnesses, facsimiles, access conditions, and rights assertions remain distinct.

catalogSummary.author: Laȝamon (also modernized as Layamon or Lawman)
catalogSummary.date: Probably later twelfth century, and before 1216 at the latest; exact composition date debated
catalogSummary.editor: G. L. Brook and R. F. Leslie
catalogSummary.region: West Midlands, associated through the prologue with Areley Kings near Worcester; the Caligula witness was copied in the south-west Midlands
catalogSummary.title: Laȝamon’s Brut (Caligula version)

catalogingSubject.label: Laȝamon’s Brut (Caligula version)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the longer, more conservative Caligula version of Laȝamon’s Middle English verse history as represented by British Library Cotton MS Caligula A IX and by the 16,096-line Brook-Leslie/U-M stream. It is not the abstract work embracing both versions, the substantially abridged and reworked Otho version, either physical codex, the 1847 or 1963-1978 editions, the OTA deposit, the U-M transformation, or the General Corpus XML artifact. The XML's 161 supplied 100-line groups and 418 printed-page milestones are navigation inherited from digital and edition layers, not authorial divisions.

contentParts.label: Laȝamon’s Brut, Caligula version
notes: No lower-level authorial contents structure is asserted. The XML's 161 supplied divisions are intentionally not represented as 161 intellectual parts.
contentParts.summary: The complete Caligula-version stream represented by the repository encoding, from Laȝamon's prologue and Trojan origins through the close of British rule.

dateStatements.display: Probably later twelfth century; before 1216 at the latest
notes: The repository header's single date 1205 is retained as legacy metadata but is not promoted to a securely established year. The normalized range follows the broader current scholarly statement.

dateStatements.display: Second half of the thirteenth century (1250-1299), in Cotton MS Caligula A IX
notes: This is the date of the earliest surviving copy modeled for this version, not the composition date. Both surviving manuscripts are substantially later than composition.

extent.description: One counted verse work. The repository XML contains 16,096 unique consecutively numbered L elements, 161 DIV1 elements labeled as supplied line groups, 418 PB elements keyed to the source edition, and a final Amen in a CLOSER. The 16,096-line total and supplied 100-line groups are encoding/edition navigation, not a claim of authorial sectioning or universally canonical lineation.

formStatements.description: A long verse history of slightly over 16,000 long lines. Long lines divide into half-lines and are linked variably by alliteration or rhyme; the Caligula manuscript's punctus elevatus signals the half-line division. The repository encoding represents 16,096 numbered L elements.

genres.label: Historiography
notes: The poem reshapes legendary and historical traditions; the label does not imply modern critical historiography.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: More specifically Early Middle English. Latin title forms and the modern English editorial layer do not make the medieval poem itself multilingual.
The Caligula and Otho texts are versions of one abstract work but materially different textual streams; this record deliberately catalogs only Caligula.

The Caligula manuscript's copy date and region, the Brook-Leslie edition's publication dates, the 1992 OTA deposit date, and the 1993 U-M date describe different layers and are not merged into the work's composition date.
The 16,096 numbered lines are a practical locator inherited from the immediate edition and encoding, not a claim that the manuscript itself numbers or structures the poem this way.
Image availability, image-file reuse terms, modern-edition copyright, and digital-encoding rights are recorded by component in the lineage manifest; none is inferred from the medieval text's age.

next_steps: Consult recent source studies and a current critical introduction.
Keep the broad 1150-1215 normalization and distinguish named works from hypothetical physical exemplars.
Map any additional source only at passages for which direct scholarship supports it.
openQuestions.question: Can the composition date be narrowed, and what exact English and Latin works or exemplars underlie Laȝamon's prologue claim beyond the dominant use of Wace's Roman de Brut?

next_steps: Inspect Otho directly if a complete facsimile becomes available.
Consult codicological descriptions of the Otho manuscript.
Retain Libri Brutonum as an unassigned manuscript-era title form until a precise witness-level citation is secured.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact Otho-witness title rubric and location associated in scholarship with the alternate form Libri Brutonum?

next_steps: Review Brook and Leslie's introductions and the Caligula rubric sequence.
Do not convert the 161 supplied XML line groups into chapters.
Add stable intellectual parts only when manuscript or editorial evidence establishes their boundaries.
openQuestions.question: What large-scale divisions, if any, can be attributed to the medieval work rather than to Brook-Leslie lineation or U-M navigation?

notes: This is a broad linguistic and cultural region; it does not prove a precise place of composition.
The prologue locates Laȝamon at Ernleȝe; the modern identification with Areley Kings is scholarly and catalog-based.
The narrative ranges beyond Britain but organizes a legendary history of the Britons from Trojan origins to the end of British rule.
This statement describes the production region of the Caligula witness, not automatically the work's place of composition.

The self-identification in the prologue and current cataloging support the attribution; the exact historical process of composition remains partly recoverable only through the poem's own source account and later comparison.

The immediate and dominant literary relationship is to Wace's Roman de Brut. The prologue also claims use of English and Latin books, but their exact identities and passage-level contributions are not treated as established here.
Brook's responsibility belongs to the edition layer linked in lineage.

scope.description: Editorial responsibility for the 1963-1978 immediate source edition, not medieval authorship

notes: Leslie's responsibility belongs to the edition layer linked in lineage.

scope.description: Editorial responsibility for the 1963-1978 immediate source edition, not medieval authorship

subjects.label: Legendary history of Britain and the Britons

scope.description: The extensive Arthurian reign, wars, and fall within the larger Brut history

summaries.value: Laȝamon recounts the legendary history of Britain from the aftermath of Troy and Brutus's foundation of the island through successive rulers, invasions, conversions, civil wars, Arthur's rise and fall, and the ending of British rule. Working chiefly from Wace's Roman de Brut while claiming other English and Latin books, the poet expands, localizes, and reshapes the Brut tradition in a long Early Middle English verse narrative whose long lines combine alliterative and rhyming techniques.

This record describes the Caligula version only. Cotton Caligula A IX is its principal and most complete witness. Cotton Otho C XIII transmits a later, abridged and reworked version and remains a related source rather than a coequal witness to every Caligula reading. Brook and Leslie's edition prints both streams; the OTA and U-M item selected the Caligula stream and omitted edition front matter, back matter, notes, and a facsimile.

notes: The parenthetical version label is supplied for discovery and prevents the materially different Otho version from being collapsed into this record.
The historical institution name and shelfmark punctuation are retained exactly as the U-M discovery title; the current holding institution is the British Library.
titles.value: Layamon's Brut (British Museum Ms. Cotton Caligula A.IX)

notes: Goering identifies Brut as the modern conventional title, not a title proven to have been supplied by Laȝamon.
Cotton Caligula A IX f. 3r visibly opens with the red rubric ‘Incipit historia brutonum.’ The normalized title omits the incipit formula.
A manuscript-era title form reported in current scholarship; this review did not assign it to a specific surviving rubric or witness.
